en.extract:
The pale-footed bush warbler (Hemitesia pallidipes) is a species of oriental warbler in the family Cettiidae that is found in southern Asia. It occurs in the Himalayan region west from Dehradun through the foothills of Nepal to northeastern India. It also occurs in Myanmar, Laos, northern Vietnam and southern China. A single sighting was recorded from Kandy, Sri Lanka in March 1993.


Taxonomy
The pale-footed bush warbler was formally described in 1872 by the English naturalist William Blandford under the binomial name Phylloscopus pallidipes. The specific epithet pallidipes combines the Latin pallidus meaning "pale" with pes meaning "foot". This warbler in now placed in the genus Hemitesia that was introduced by James Chapin in 1948.
Three subspecies are recognised:

H. p. pallidipes (Blanford, 1872)	– Himalayas to west Yunnan (south China) and north Myanmar
H. p. laurentei La Touche, 1921 – central Myanmar and south east Yunnan (south China) to Vietnam
H. p. osmastoni (Hartert, E, 1908) – Andaman Islands


Distribution and habitat
The pale-footed bush warbler usually associates with Themeda grasslands. The habitat and altitudinal limit for pale-footed bush warbler vary slightly from place to place. For example, in Thailand, its habitat is grassland and scrub from foothills up to 1,800 m (5,900 ft). In China, it is found in woodland up to 1,525 m (5,003 ft).


Nepal
The pale-footed bush warbler is a resident bird for Nepal. It is mostly found in Chitwan.  This species is threatened by habitat loss and population decline in Nepal.


India
In peninsular India the pale-footed bush warbler U. p. pallidipes has been reported as breeding in the Eastern Ghats by Salim Ali and Sidney Dillon Ripley. They collected a specimen from Sunkarametta, Araku Valley, Vishakapatnam district [altitude of 1,000 m (3,300 ft)] in the month of April. A male adult of the subspecies U. p. osmastoni was first described from Andaman Islands [altitude 730 m (2,400 ft)] by Ernst Hartert. Nest-building in U. p. osmastoni was described by Bertram Beresford Osmaston in the year 1907. Subspecies U. p. pallidipes has also been reported from Simlipal Tiger Reserve, Simlipal National Park Odisha [altitude 600–1,500 m (2,000–4,900 ft)] in December 2012 from a grassland near a Sal forest


Behaviour
The pale-footed bush warbler is extremely shy and is a great skulker, meaning it is very difficult to see even during the breeding season. This species breeds from May to July. The bird is usually found either by itself or in pairs in low bushes and grass clumps. It is also known as a ground-dwelling species; thus, it flies less than a meter above ground. It moves through grass reeds keeping low, staying mostly out of sight and it feeds on the lower half of grass reeds.


Vocalization
The pale-footed bush warbler has a loud song. Because it remains near the ground, it is more easily heard than seen. This species remains mostly silent during winter but its call and song can be easily heard during spring. Its explosive song is the best way to signal the presence of its species.


References

The '''pale-footed bush warbler''' ('''''Hemitesia pallidipes''''') is a species of oriental [[warbler]] in the [[Family (taxonomy)|family]] [[Cettiidae]] that is found in southern [[Asia]]. It occurs in the Himalayan region west from [[Dehradun]] through the foothills of [[Nepal]] to northeastern [[India]]. It also occurs in [[Myanmar]], [[Laos]], northern [[Vietnam]] and southern [[China]].<ref name="Baral 2009 228"/> A single sighting was recorded from [[Kandy]], [[Sri Lanka]] in March 1993.

==Taxonomy==
The pale-footed bush warbler was [[species description|formally described]] in 1872 by the English naturalist [[William Thomas Blanford|William Blandford]] under the [[binomial name]] ''Phylloscopus pallidipes''.<ref>{{cite journal | last=Blanford | first=William | author-link=William Thomas Blanford | date=1872 | title=Notes on a collection of birds from Sikkim | journal=Journal of the Asiatic Society of Bengal | volume=41 | issue=2 | pages=152–170 [162] | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/37137878 }}</ref> The specific epithet ''pallidipes'' combines the [[Latin]] ''pallidus'' meaning "pale" with ''pes'' meaning "foot".<ref>{{cite book | last=Jobling | first=James A. | year=2010| title=The Helm Dictionary of Scientific Bird Names | publisher=Christopher Helm | location=London | isbn=978-1-4081-2501-4 | page=289 | url=https://archive.org/stream/Helm_Dictionary_of_Scientific_Bird_Names_by_James_A._Jobling#page/n289/mode/1up }}</ref> This warbler in now placed in the genus ''[[Hemitesia]]'' that was introduced by [[James Chapin]] in 1948.<ref name=ioc>{{cite web| editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen | date=August 2022 | title=Cupwings, crombecs, bush warblers, Streaked Scrub Warbler, yellow flycatchers, hylias | work=IOC World Bird List Version 12.2 | url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/crombecs/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=26 August 2022 }}</ref>

Three [[subspecies]] are recognised:<ref name=ioc/>
* ''H. p. pallidipes'' ([[William Thomas Blanford|Blanford]], 1872)	– Himalayas to west [[Yunnan]] (south China) and north [[Myanmar]]
* ''H. p. laurentei'' [[John David Digues La Touche|La Touche]], 1921 – central Myanmar and south east Yunnan (south China) to Vietnam
* ''H. p. osmastoni'' ([[Ernst Hartert|Hartert, E]], 1908) – [[Andaman Islands]]

[[File:Pale-footed Bush Warbler.ogg|thumb|Call of pale-footed bush warbler]]
[[File:Sonogram of Urosphena pallidipes call.png|thumb|Sonogram of pale-footed bush warbler]]

== Distribution and habitat==
The pale-footed bush warbler usually associates with ''[[Themeda triandra|Themeda]]'' grasslands. The habitat and altitudinal limit for pale-footed bush warbler vary slightly from place to place. For example, in Thailand, its habitat is grassland and scrub from foothills up to {{cvt|1800|m}}. In China, it is found in woodland up to {{cvt|1525|m}}.<ref name="Baral 2009 228"/>

===Nepal===
The pale-footed bush warbler is a resident bird for Nepal. It is mostly found in Chitwan.  This species is threatened by habitat loss and population decline in Nepal.<ref name="Baral 2009 228"/>

===India===
In peninsular India the pale-footed bush warbler ''U. p. pallidipes'' has been reported as breeding in the [[Eastern Ghats]] by [[Salim Ali]] and [[Sidney Dillon Ripley]]. They collected a specimen from Sunkarametta, [[Araku Valley]], [[Vishakapatnam]] district [altitude of {{cvt|1000|m}}] in the month of April.<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://archive.org/details/in.ernet.dli.2015.120814|title=Compact Handbook Of The Birds Of India And Pakistan Second Edition|last=Dillon Ripley &|first=Salim Ali|date=1907}}</ref> A male adult of the subspecies ''U. p. osmastoni'' was first described from [[Andaman Islands]] [altitude {{cvt|730|m}}] by [[Ernst Hartert]].<ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/100926|title=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club.|last1=British Ornithologists' Club.|last2=Club|first2=British Ornithologists'|date=1907–1909|publisher=British Ornithologists' Club|volume=v.21-23=no.136-153 (1907-1909)|location=London }}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/item/18938|title=The journal of the Bombay Natural History Society.|last1=Bombay Natural History Society.|last2=Society|first2=Bombay Natural History|date=1907–1908|publisher=Bombay Natural History Society|volume=v.18 (1907-1908)|location=Bombay }}</ref> Nest-building in ''U. p. osmastoni'' was described by [[Bertram Beresford Osmaston]] in the year 1907.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Osmaston|first=Bertram Beresford|date=January 1932|title=Some Andaman Birds|url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/47859114#page/565/mode/1up|journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society|volume=35|pages=891–892}}</ref> Subspecies ''U. p. pallidipes'' has also been reported from Simlipal Tiger Reserve, [[Simlipal National Park]] [[Odisha]] [altitude {{cvt|600|-|1500|m}}] in December 2012 from a grassland near a Sal forest<ref>{{Cite journal|last=J Praveen &|first=Dipu Karuthedathu|date=December 2012|title=Some significant avian records from Odisha|url=https://indianbirds.in/pdfs/IB9.1.KaruthedathuETAL_Odisha.pdf|journal=Indian Birds|volume= 9| issue =  1 (Publ. 17 JaNuary 2014)|page=17}}</ref>

==Behaviour==
The pale-footed bush warbler is extremely shy and is a great skulker, meaning it is very difficult to see even during the breeding season. This species breeds from May to July. The bird is usually found either by itself or in pairs in low bushes and grass clumps. It is also known as a ground-dwelling species; thus, it flies less than a meter above ground. It moves through grass reeds keeping low, staying mostly out of sight and it feeds on the lower half of grass reeds.<ref name="Baral 2009 228">{{cite journal|last=Baral|first=H.S|title=Pale-footed Bush- warbler Cettia pallidipes in Nepal with Reference to its Global Status and Distribution|journal=Our Nature|date=12 September 2009|volume=7|pages=226–230|doi=10.3126/on.v7i1.2577|doi-access=free}}</ref>

===Vocalization===
The pale-footed bush warbler has a loud song. Because it remains near the ground, it is more easily heard than seen. This species remains mostly silent during winter but its call and song can be easily heard during spring. Its explosive song is the best way to signal the presence of its species.<ref name="Baral 2009 228"/>

zh.extract:
淡脚树莺（学名：Urosphena pallidipes）为樹鶯科短尾莺属的鸟类。该物种分布在南亚地区，其模式产地在锡金。
2011年，基于系统发育研究的结果，淡脚树莺由短尾莺属（Urosphena）移入Hemitesia属（Alström et al. 2011）。


亚种
淡脚树莺云南亚种（学名：Urosphena pallidipes laurentei）。在中国大陆，分布于广东等地。该物种的模式产地在云南。
淡脚树莺指名亚种（学名：Urosphena pallidipes pallidipes）。在中国大陆，分布于云南等地。该物种的模式产地在锡金。
